questions.specification: The effectiveness of web search and the speed of content delivery depend on technical standards and optimization strategies. Website administrators use various tools to guide search engines, while modern image formats help maintain a balance between visual quality and network performance.

parts.content: Outline the specific role of each of the following in optimizing a website for search engines: Robots.txt files, XML Sitemaps, and meta tags (e.g., title and description).
parts.markscheme:
- **Robots.txt**: State that it provides instructions to web crawlers about which directories or files should not be scanned. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Robots.txt**: Explain that it can discourage crawling of duplicate content or private administrative pages. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **XML Sitemaps**: State that it provides a structured list of all URLs on a website to help crawlers discover pages. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **XML Sitemaps**: Explain how it can indicate site structure and/or the relative importance/update frequency of pages. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Meta tags (title/description)**: Identify that they provide page-level metadata used by search engines to understand the page’s topic. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Meta tags (title/description)**: Describe how clear, relevant titles/descriptions can improve how a result appears in search listings and can increase click-through rate. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how a search engine can use a web crawler and an index to retrieve relevant pages for a user’s query.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe that a **web crawler** automatically follows links to discover/download web pages. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that discovered pages are processed to extract content/keywords and links. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe that an **index** stores information about pages (e.g., terms mapped to pages) to enable fast lookup. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how 'personalized search' can create a 'filter bubble' for a user, and describe the impact this has on the variety of information retrieved.
parts.markscheme:
- State that personalized search uses a user's previous history, location, and cookies to tailor search results. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Define a 'filter bubble' as a state of intellectual isolation where the algorithm mainly shows content that aligns with the user's existing interests/beliefs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that this reduces the variety of information by down-ranking/hiding perspectives that challenge or contradict the user. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe how this can reinforce confirmation bias and prevent users from discovering new or diverse topics. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) versus WebP image formats in enhancing a website's performance and user experience.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe SVG as a vector format based on mathematical paths that allows for infinite scaling without loss of quality. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that SVG is ideal for simple graphics like logos or icons, as it typically results in very small file sizes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe WebP as a modern photographic format that offers superior lossy and lossless compression compared to JPEG/PNG. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that using WebP reduces the total page weight, leading to faster loading times for the user on low-bandwidth connections. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Discuss how choosing the correct format (SVG for clarity at any size, WebP for high-detail photos) optimizes the balance between visual aesthetics and technical performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A company uses graph theory to improve its web portal structure, ensuring better connectivity between sections.

parts.content: Explain the term connectivity as it applies to web design.
parts.markscheme:
- State that connectivity refers to the existence of paths between different pages (nodes) in the website's graph structure <marks>R1</marks>

- Explain that high connectivity allows for seamless user navigation between various sections, ensuring all content is accessible <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define Connectivity Using Graph Theory
steps.content:
To understand connectivity in a web context, we first look at it through the lens of graph theory (which you can study further in **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**). 

In formal terms, a graph consists of **nodes** (or vertices) and **edges**. **Connectivity** refers to the existence of **paths** between these different nodes in the graph structure. If a path exists between two nodes, it means you can travel from one to the other through a sequence of edges.

highlights.text: existence of paths between different pages (nodes) in the website's graph structure

steps.title: Translate Theory to Web Design
steps.content:
When we apply this theory to a website, we treat each individual **web page** as a node and each **hyperlink** as an edge. 

In this context, connectivity means that there are clear navigational routes (links) that allow a user to move from one page to another. A "well-connected" web portal is one where the graph structure ensures that no page is isolated and every section can be reached from the home page or other relevant areas.

steps.title: Practical Result: User Navigation
steps.content:
The practical application of high connectivity is **seamless user navigation**. 

By ensuring paths exist between nodes, the company makes sure that all content is accessible to the user. This reduces the number of clicks needed to find information and prevents "dead ends," where a user might get stuck on a page with no way to return to the main sections. This aligns with the principles of **usability** found in **Topic 1.2: System design basics**.

highlights.text: high connectivity allows for seamless user navigation between various sections, ensuring all content is accessible

steps.title: Focus on the functional goal
steps.content:
In web design, the primary functional goal is **accessibility**. For a user, connectivity represents the ability to reach any piece of information on a website from any other point. If a site has poor connectivity, users might find "dead ends" or content that is impossible to reach without manually typing a URL, which creates a poor user experience.

This aligns with **Topic 1.2.11 (Usability and Accessibility)**, where we focus on ensuring systems are designed to be used effectively by all stakeholders.

highlights.text: connectivity as it applies to web design

steps.title: Model as a graph structure
steps.content:
To achieve this goal of accessibility, we model the website as a **directed graph**. 

In this structure:
- **Nodes** ($V$) represent individual web pages.
- **Edges** ($E$) represent the hyperlinks connecting them.

Connectivity is formally defined as the **existence of a path** (a sequence of links) between different nodes. If a path exists from page $A$ to page $B$, those pages are considered connected. This is a core concept from **Topic C.5.1 (Web as a Directed Graph)**.

steps.title: Achieving seamless navigation
steps.content:
The final part of our explanation focuses on the quality of these connections. **High connectivity** means that the graph is well-linked, allowing for **seamless navigation**.

When a web portal has high connectivity, a user can move between various sections efficiently. This ensures that the "functional goal" we identified in Step 0 is met: all content remains accessible through a logical and connected graph of links, rather than being isolated in silos.

parts.content: Outline two ways graph theory insights can improve user experience on a website.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify well-connected pages (nodes with high degree centrality) to optimize navigation menus and prioritize access to key information <marks>M1A1</marks>

- Detect isolated or "orphan" pages (nodes with no incoming edges) that need additional links to improve their discoverability <marks>M1A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award 2 marks for each way outlined: 1 mark for identifying the graph theory insight and 1 mark for explaining the improvement to user experience. Other valid points include clustering similar content or minimizing the shortest path length between critical pages.</note>

parts.content: Describe one challenge of maintaining connectivity on a website that frequently updates.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that frequent additions or removals of pages (nodes) and links (edges) can disrupt the existing network structure <marks>M1</marks>

- Explain that these changes can create broken links or disconnected components, making certain sections of the site unreachable <marks>A1</marks>

- Describe the increased maintenance complexity required to continuously audit and verify the integrity of the graph structure <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Model the website's structure
steps.content:
To understand this challenge through **Structural Connectivity Analysis**, we first view the website as a directed graph $G = (V, E)$. Here, the pages are vertices (nodes $V$) and the hyperlinks are directed edges (links $E$). 

This perspective is covered in **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**, which explores how graph theory applies to web connectivity.

steps.title: Disruption of the graph structure
steps.content:
The primary challenge is that frequent updates involve the constant addition or removal of nodes ($V$) and edges ($E$). 

When a page is deleted without updating the pages that link to it, or when new pages are added without proper outgoing links, the existing network structure is disrupted. This volatility makes it difficult to maintain a stable topology.

highlights.text: Identify that frequent additions or removals of pages (nodes) and links (edges) can disrupt the existing network structure

steps.title: Creation of disconnected components
steps.content:
In structural terms, removing a 'bridge' edge or a central node can break the paths between different parts of the site. 

This leads to the creation of **disconnected components** or **isolated nodes**. Effectively, these are 'broken links' where a user can no longer find a path from the homepage to a specific section, making those parts of the website unreachable.

highlights.text: Explain that these changes can create broken links or disconnected components, making certain sections of the site unreachable

steps.title: Increased maintenance and auditing
steps.content:
Maintaining connectivity requires constant auditing to verify the integrity of the graph. 

As the site grows and changes, the complexity of ensuring every node remains reachable increases. Developers must use automated tools to perform crawl-tests, ensuring that the 'strongly connected' nature of the web portal is preserved despite the high frequency of updates.

highlights.text: Describe the increased maintenance complexity required to continuously audit and verify the integrity of the graph structure

steps.title: Structural Disruption
steps.content:
In graph theory, a website is viewed as a **directed graph** where pages are nodes and hyperlinks are edges. A major challenge with frequent updates is that the constant addition or removal of these nodes and edges can disrupt the established network structure. 

This relates to **Topic 1.1.11 (Managing Software Releases and Updates)**, where change management is crucial for maintaining system stability.

highlights.text: frequent additions or removals of pages (nodes) and links (edges) can disrupt the existing network structure

steps.title: Reachability Failures
steps.content: When nodes are removed without updating all corresponding edges, or when new pages are added without proper pathing, the graph's **reachability** is compromised. This results in "broken links" (edges pointing to non-existent nodes) or "disconnected components," where sections of the web portal become isolated from the main navigation, making them unreachable for the user.

highlights.text: broken links or disconnected components, making certain sections of the site unreachable

steps.title: Verification and Auditing Complexity
steps.content: From an administrative standpoint, the primary challenge is the **increased complexity of auditing**. As the graph grows and changes, the resources required to continuously verify the integrity of every path increase significantly. Ensuring that no page has become an "island" requires systematic verification tools and manual oversight to maintain a seamless user experience.

highlights.text: increased maintenance complexity required to continuously audit and verify the integrity of the graph structure

questions.specification: Search engines rely on web crawlers to systematically browse and index the web for relevant content.

parts.content: Define a web crawler.
parts.markscheme:
- A web crawler is a program that systematically browses the World Wide Web to gather and index page data <marks>1</marks>

- It is used by search engines to create and maintain a comprehensive index of the internet's content <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the web crawler
steps.content:
To define a web crawler technically, we start by identifying its nature: it is an **automated program** (often called a 'spider' or 'bot') that operates autonomously on the internet. 

Its core function is to **systematically browse** the World Wide Web by following hyperlinks from one page to another to discover and gather information.

highlights.text: systematically browse

steps.title: Explain the indexing purpose
steps.content:
The information gathered by the crawler is used by search engines to create and maintain a **comprehensive index** of the internet's content. 

Think of this index as a massive digital library catalogue; without the crawler's work in organizing this data, a search engine wouldn't be able to provide **relevant content** to users quickly.

highlights.text: index the web for relevant content

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn the **2 marks** by including both the action and the purpose:

1.  **Action:** An automated program that systematically browses the WWW to gather page data.
2.  **Purpose:** Used by search engines to create and maintain a searchable index.

parts.content: Explain how a web crawler uses the web graph to identify and index new pages.
parts.markscheme:
- The crawler starts with a list of known "seed" pages and follows the hyperlinks found within them to discover new URLs <marks>1</marks>

- It utilizes the web graph structure, where web pages are represented as nodes and hyperlinks are directed edges, allowing the crawler to navigate between related content <marks>1</marks>

- By recording these interconnections and adding metadata from the newly found pages, the crawler builds and updates the search engine's index <marks>1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Outline two limitations that web crawlers might face in attempting to index the entire web.
parts.markscheme:
- Restricted access to the "Deep Web": many pages are password-protected, behind firewalls, or require user authentication <marks>1</marks>, which prevents public crawlers from accessing and indexing that content <marks>1</marks>

- Resource and scale constraints: the massive volume of data requires significant bandwidth and storage <marks>1</marks>, and the high frequency of content changes makes it difficult to maintain an index that is completely current <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award [2 marks] for each limitation clearly outlined, up to a maximum of [4 marks]. Other acceptable points include robots.txt exclusions or issues with duplicate content.</note>

methods.label: Focus on Restricted Access and Protocols

steps.title: Identify Web Crawler Limitations
steps.content: To answer this question, we need to focus on the technical and protocol-based barriers that stop a web crawler from seeing every page. In **Topic C.2: Searching the Web**, we learn that while crawlers are powerful, they are bound by security constraints and administrative rules set by website owners.

highlights.text: index the entire web

steps.title: Limitation 1: The Deep Web
steps.content:
The first major limitation is restricted access to the **Deep Web**. This refers to parts of the internet that are not reachable via standard links. 

*   **Barrier:** Many pages are behind firewalls, require password-protected user authentication, or are stored in private databases.
*   **Impact:** Since crawlers are automated scripts that do not possess user credentials, they are blocked from accessing and indexing this content, leaving a huge portion of the web invisible to search engines.

highlights.text: Restricted access to the "Deep Web": many pages are password-protected, behind firewalls, or require user authentication

steps.title: Limitation 2: robots.txt Protocols
steps.content:
The second limitation involves administrative protocols, specifically the **robots.txt** exclusion file. 

*   **Barrier:** Website administrators can place a file named `robots.txt` in their root directory to specify which parts of their site should not be visited by crawlers.
*   **Impact:** Ethical web crawlers (like those from Google or Bing) are programmed to follow these instructions. If a site owner "disallows" a directory, the crawler will skip it entirely, preventing those pages from being indexed.

highlights.text: robots.txt exclusions

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure the full 4 marks, ensure you clearly state the limitation and its direct effect on the indexing process:

1.  **Deep Web Access:** Authentication requirements (passwords/firewalls) prevent crawlers from accessing private data (2 marks).
2.  **Exclusion Protocols:** The `robots.txt` file allows webmasters to explicitly tell crawlers to stay away from certain content, which the crawlers respect (2 marks).

methods.label: Focus on Logistical and Temporal Constraints

steps.title: Analyze the Task
steps.content: To earn **4 marks**, we need to outline two distinct limitations that prevent web crawlers from indexing the 100% of the web. We will focus specifically on the **logistical** (physical resources) and **temporal** (timing/frequency) constraints involved in such a massive task.

steps.title: Logistical: Physical Resource Constraints
steps.content:
The first major limitation is the sheer scale of the web. Indexing billions of pages requires immense physical resources:

*   **Bandwidth:** Crawlers must download page content to process it. The cumulative bandwidth required to constantly fetch every single page on the web is often beyond the capacity of even large search providers.
*   **Storage:** Once crawled, the data must be stored in a searchable index. Storing petabytes of data is a significant logistical challenge in terms of hardware costs and server maintenance.

highlights.text: The massive volume of data requires significant bandwidth and storage

steps.title: Temporal: Frequency of Change
steps.content:
The second limitation is **temporal** (time-based). The web is dynamic, with content changing every second:

*   **Currency of Index:** By the time a crawler finishes indexing a segment of the web, many of the pages it first visited have already been updated or deleted. 
*   **Synchronization:** There is a constant lag between a change occurring on a live website and that change appearing in the search engine's index. This makes it impossible to maintain a 100% current index of the entire web at any single point in time.

highlights.text: the high frequency of content changes makes it difficult to maintain an index that is completely current

steps.title: Alternative: Access Constraints
steps.content: Another logistical barrier is the **Deep Web**. Many pages are physically unreachable because they are protected by passwords, firewalls, or require user authentication. Since a crawler cannot "log in" or bypass security layers, these pages remain invisible to the indexing process, regardless of how much bandwidth the crawler has.

highlights.text: Restricted access to the "Deep Web": many pages are password-protected, behind firewalls, or require user authentication

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To get full marks, ensure you describe the limitation and its consequence:

1.  **Scale/Resources:** The massive volume of data requires significant bandwidth and storage, which limits how much can be physically indexed.
2.  **Temporal/Dynamic:** High frequency of content changes makes it difficult to keep the index up to date, leading to stale or missing information.

questions.specification: SocialMediaMap is a platform that clusters web pages and content based on user interests and frequently updated social tags.

parts.content: Define a sub-graph in the context of web analysis.
parts.markscheme:
Award **[1]** for each of the following up to **[2]**:
- A sub-graph is a graph whose set of vertices and edges are subsets of a larger graph <marks>1</marks>
- In web analysis, it represents a specific collection of related web pages or topic clusters within the broader internet network <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define the mathematical structure
steps.content:
Using the **Structural Graph Theory Approach**, we define a sub-graph by its relationship to a parent graph. 

If we have a larger graph $G$ representing the entire network, a sub-graph $G'$ is defined such that its set of vertices ($V'$) and edges ($E'$) are subsets of the vertices ($V$) and edges ($E$) of the parent graph.

$$\begin{aligned} V' &\subseteq V \\ E' &\subseteq E \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: A sub-graph is a graph whose set of vertices and edges are subsets of a larger graph

steps.title: Apply to web analysis
steps.content:
In the context of web analysis (like the SocialMediaMap platform), the "parent graph" represents the entire internet or a large database of web pages. 

A sub-graph identifies a specific subset of these pages that are linked together, forming a **topic cluster** or a community based on shared user interests or social tags.

highlights.text: In web analysis, it represents a specific collection of related web pages or topic clusters within the broader internet network

steps.title: Synthesize the definition
steps.content:
To earn both marks, ensure your definition includes both the structural requirement and the application:

1. **Structure:** A sub-graph is a collection of vertices and edges that are entirely contained within a larger graph.
2. **Web Context:** It represents a specific segment of the web, such as a cluster of pages related by specific social tags or user interests.

steps.title: Define the mathematical sub-graph
steps.content:
To understand a sub-graph, we first look at it through the lens of graph theory. A graph is made up of a set of vertices (nodes) and edges (the connections between them). 

A **sub-graph** is defined as a graph whose set of vertices and set of edges are both subsets of a larger, parent graph. In simpler terms, you are taking a 'piece' of the bigger network without adding any new connections that weren't already there.

highlights.text: A sub-graph is a graph whose set of vertices and edges are subsets of a larger graph

steps.title: Apply to web analysis
steps.content:
When we apply this to the World Wide Web, the 'parent graph' is the entire internet. In the **Topical Clustering Approach**, we don't just look at random pages; we look for a **sub-graph** that forms a distinct community.

In this context, a sub-graph represents a specific collection of related web pages or **topic clusters** (like a group of sites all about 'Climate Change' or 'IB Revision') within the broader internet network. These pages are linked together because they share a common theme or interest.

highlights.text: In web analysis, it represents a specific collection of related web pages or topic clusters within the broader internet network

steps.title: Review for marks
steps.content:
To ensure you get both marks in an IB exam, your definition must include:
1. The formal definition: Vertices and edges are **subsets** of a larger graph.
2. The contextual application: It represents a **cluster or community** of pages related by a common theme.

This concept is a key part of **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**, where we treat the web as a directed graph to understand how information is organized.

parts.content: Explain two reasons why SocialMediaMap might use sub-graphs to organize content for users.

steps.title: Analyze System Requirements
steps.content:
SocialMediaMap functions by organizing a massive amount of data into clusters. From a **System-Oriented Approach**, we look at how the structure of data (using **sub-graphs** instead of the entire **global web graph**) impacts the performance and efficiency of the platform's algorithms. 

In the IB Computer Science syllabus (**Topic C.5.2**), we distinguish between the global web graph and its sub-graphs to understand how specific niches can be managed more effectively.

highlights.text: clusters web pages and content based on user interests and frequently updated social tags

steps.title: Reason 1: Computational Efficiency
steps.content:
The primary technical benefit of using sub-graphs is **computational efficiency**. 

Processing the entire global web graph is extremely resource-intensive. By restricting search and recommendation algorithms to a smaller **sub-graph**, the system reduces the **search space**. This reduction allows the platform to achieve **lower latency** and perform **real-time content updates**, which is critical for a platform relying on "frequently updated social tags."

**Marking Note:** You earn 1 mark for identifying efficiency and 1 mark for explaining the impact on speed/latency.

highlights.text: Search and recommendation algorithms are much faster when restricted to a smaller sub-graph than when processing the entire global web graph
which allows for real-time content updates and lower latency

steps.title: Reason 2: Targeted Relevance
steps.content:
Another reason is ensuring **targeted content and relevance**. 

By clustering content into sub-graphs based on specific user interests (folksonomies/social tags), the system effectively filters out **"noise"** from unrelated topics. From a system design perspective, this ensures that the data being processed is highly relevant to the specific user's query, improving the precision of the output and the overall user experience.

**Marking Note:** You earn 1 mark for identifying relevance/targeting and 1 mark for explaining how it filters noise to improve experience.

highlights.text: Clustering content into sub-graphs based on social tags allows the platform to provide highly relevant content to users
improving the user experience by filtering out noise from unrelated topics

steps.title: Understanding sub-graphs
steps.content:
In the context of the web, the entire internet can be seen as one giant directed graph. A **sub-graph** is a smaller, isolated portion of that graph. For **SocialMediaMap**, these sub-graphs are created by grouping content that shares specific social tags or user interests. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**, specifically looking at how we can break down the massive global web graph into manageable pieces.

highlights.text: clusters web pages and content based on user interests and frequently updated social tags

steps.title: Reason 1: Content Relevance
steps.content:
The first reason is to provide **targeted content**. By clustering content into sub-graphs based on social tags, the platform ensures that users only see what is relevant to their specific interests. 

From a human-centric perspective, this significantly improves the user experience by **filtering out 'noise'** (unrelated or spammy content) that would otherwise clutter their feed. This makes the information much more digestible and useful for the end-user.

steps.title: Reason 2: Visual Navigation
steps.content:
The second reason is to **improve visualization and navigation**. A global web graph is far too complex for a human to look at and understand. By using a sub-graph, the platform can display a simplified map that highlights the most important nodes and relationships within a specific niche. 

This allows users to easily identify **key trends or influencers** without being overwhelmed by millions of unrelated data points, making the interface much more intuitive to navigate.

steps.title: Final check of marks
steps.content:
To get full marks on an IB exam, you need to provide a clear reason and then explain the benefit:

1. **Targeted Content:** Clustering by tags provides relevant info **[1]** and filters out noise to improve user experience **[1]**.
2. **Improved Visualization:** Showing a sub-graph highlights important nodes in a niche **[1]** and makes it easier to identify trends without clutter **[1]**.

parts.content: Describe one limitation of using sub-graphs to group related web pages.

steps.title: Identify the clustering method
steps.content: In the context of Web Science, the platform uses algorithms to partition the larger web graph into smaller **sub-graphs**. These sub-graphs represent clusters of web pages that share similar social tags or user interests, effectively creating specialized "neighborhoods" of information.

highlights.text: SocialMediaMap is a platform that clusters web pages and content based on user interests and frequently updated social tags.

steps.title: Recognize Information Isolation
steps.content: A significant limitation of this approach is the creation of **filter bubbles**. Because the platform restricts a user's view primarily to a specific sub-graph, they are effectively isolated from the rest of the web graph. This means they are only exposed to content that aligns with their existing profile, leading to information isolation.

highlights.text: Users may only be exposed to content within their specific sub-graph

steps.title: Evaluate the social impact
steps.content:
This isolation results in a **lack of diversity**. When users are confined to a sub-graph, they lose the opportunity to encounter diverse perspectives or discover new topics that fall outside their current interests. This reinforces an "echo chamber" where the same ideas are constantly repeated and alternative viewpoints are hidden.

To earn full marks on the IB exam, you must clearly link the technical grouping (sub-graphs) to the social consequence (preventing discovery of diverse content).

highlights.text: prevents them from encountering diverse perspectives or discovering unrelated but potentially interesting new topics

steps.title: Define the sub-graph context
steps.content: In the context of SocialMediaMap, the web is modeled as a large directed graph where web pages are nodes and social tags represent the connections (edges) between them. A sub-graph is a smaller subset of these nodes and edges that are grouped together because they share strong commonalities or frequent updates. This is a core concept in **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**.

steps.title: Exclusion of loose connections
steps.content: The primary technical limitation of this grouping method is the **exclusion of weakly linked nodes**. Algorithms that generate sub-graphs often prioritize pages with a high density of tags or links. Consequently, web pages that are highly relevant to a topic but only loosely connected to the main cluster—perhaps because they are newer or have fewer social tags—will be left out of the sub-graph.

highlights.text: Exclusion of weakly linked nodes
loosely connected to the main cluster

steps.title: Incomplete topic representation
steps.content:
Because these weakly linked nodes are excluded, the sub-graph provides an **incomplete or biased representation** of the topic. The user only sees the most "popular" or frequently tagged content, missing out on niche but relevant information. 

In an IB exam, you would earn marks by:
1. Identifying that loose connections lead to exclusion ($1$ mark).
2. Explaining that this results in an incomplete or biased view for the user ($1$ mark).

highlights.text: incomplete or biased representation of the topic

questions.specification:
The topological structure of the World Wide Web can be analyzed using various graph theory models. Research suggests that the distribution of hyperlinks follows specific mathematical patterns, while the accessibility of content is governed by both technical protocols and the semantic organization of metadata.

As the web evolves toward ubiquitous computing and more sophisticated metadata frameworks, the interaction between automated systems and human-generated content creates new challenges for indexing and retrieval.

parts.content: Define the term *interoperability* as it applies to the hardware and software components within an ambient intelligence environment.
parts.markscheme:
- **Definition**: The ability of different systems, devices, or applications to connect and communicate with each other in a coordinated manner. <marks>1</marks>
- **Standardization**: It involves the use of common protocols or data formats (e.g., MQTT, Zigbee) to ensure that heterogeneous components can exchange information regardless of their manufacturer. <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: Distinguish between a **formal ontology** and a **folksonomy** as methods for categorizing information on the Semantic Web.
parts.markscheme:
- **Structure**: Ontologies are formal, hierarchical structures with defined relationships (is-a, part-of); folksonomies are flat, non-hierarchical structures based on tags. <marks>1</marks>
- **Creation**: Ontologies are usually created by experts or committees (top-down); folksonomies are created by users through social tagging (bottom-up). <marks>1</marks>
- **Consistency**: Ontologies provide high consistency and eliminate ambiguity (controlled vocabulary); folksonomies allow for synonymy and polysemy, which can lead to ambiguity. <marks>1</marks>
- **Scalability/Flexibility**: Folksonomies are highly scalable and adapt quickly to new trends; ontologies are rigid and difficult to update as the domain of knowledge changes. <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: The 'bow-tie' model is a significant theory in web science for describing the macroscopic structure of the web graph. Identify and describe the roles of the **IN** and **OUT** components within this model.
parts.markscheme:
- **IN component identification**: Represents a set of web pages that can reach the Strongly Connected Component (SCC) through a series of hyperlinks but cannot be reached *from* the SCC. <marks>1</marks>
- **IN role**: These are often newer pages or niche sites that link to major hubs but have not yet gained the authority to be linked back to. <marks>1</marks>
- **OUT component identification**: Represents a set of web pages that can be reached *from* the SCC but do not have any links pointing back to the SCC. <marks>1</marks>
- **OUT role**: These often consist of 'corporate' pages, static resources, or documentation pages that provide information but do not serve as navigational gateways to the rest of the web. <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: Explain **two** technical reasons why a significant portion of the World Wide Web, known as the 'Deep Web', remains invisible to standard web crawlers.
parts.markscheme:
- **Dynamic Content**: Many pages are generated 'on-the-fly' from databases in response to specific queries; crawlers cannot predict or execute all possible query combinations to find these pages. <marks>1</marks>
- **Authentication/Access Control**: Pages behind paywalls, login screens, or CAPTCHAs are inaccessible to crawlers that lack the necessary credentials or bypass mechanisms. <marks>1</marks>
- **Unlinked (Orphaned) Pages**: Crawlers navigate by following links; if a page has no incoming links from the surface web, the crawler has no path to discover it. <marks>1</marks>
- **Script-rendered content**: Content generated by client-side scripts (e.g., heavy JavaScript) may not be executed by simpler crawlers, leaving the text invisible to the indexer. <marks>1</marks>

Note: Accept references to the `robots.txt` exclusion protocol if explained as a voluntary technical barrier.

[Max 4]

parts.content: Analyze the significance of the **Power Law distribution** of web links in determining the visibility of content and the robustness of the web graph.
parts.markscheme:
- **Definition**: In a power law distribution ($$P(k) \approx k^{-\gamma}$$), a small number of 'hubs' have a very high in-degree (many links), while the vast majority of pages have very few links. <marks>1</marks>
- **Visibility (Winner-takes-all)**: This creates a 'rich-get-richer' effect (preferential attachment), where popular sites become even more visible while new sites struggle to be indexed or ranked highly. <marks>1</marks>
- **The Long Tail**: While hubs dominate traffic, the 'Long Tail' represents the collective value of millions of niche pages which, together, may contain more total information than the hubs. <marks>1</marks>
- **Robustness (Error Tolerance)**: The web is highly resilient to random failures; if a random $10$% of pages go offline, the graph remains mostly connected because most pages are not hubs. <marks>1</marks>
- **Vulnerability (Targeted Attacks)**: Conversely, the web is highly vulnerable to targeted attacks on major hubs; removing a few central nodes can significantly increase the diameter of the web or fragment the graph. <marks>1</marks>
- **Search Impact**: Search engine algorithms (like PageRank) leverage this distribution to identify authority, effectively using the power law structure to filter 'noise' from 'signal'. <marks>1</marks>

questions.specification: A news aggregator uses the concept of web diameter to understand how far apart news sources are in terms of interlinking.

parts.content:
(a) Define web diameter and explain its relevance in a web graph.

[3 marks]
parts.markscheme:
- Web diameter is the maximum shortest path (number of edges) between any two nodes within a graph <marks>1</marks>
- In the web graph, it represents the maximum "distance" or number of clicks required to travel between any two web pages <marks>1</marks>
- It helps in understanding the overall connectivity or fragmentation of sections of the web <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define via Graph Theory
steps.content:
From a formal graph theory perspective, the World Wide Web is modeled as a **directed graph** where web pages are nodes and hyperlinks are edges. 

The **web diameter** is defined as the **maximum shortest path** between any two nodes in the graph. This means you first find the shortest distance (the minimum number of edges) between every possible pair of nodes, and the largest of these values is the diameter.

highlights.text: Define web diameter

steps.title: Translate to Practical Clicks
steps.content:
In a practical web context, the diameter represents the maximum number of **clicks** a user would need to perform to navigate from one page to any other page, provided they always follow the most direct route.

If the diameter is, for example, 19, it implies that no two pages in that network are more than 19 clicks apart.

steps.title: Evaluate Connectivity and Relevance
steps.content:
The relevance of web diameter lies in measuring **connectivity**. 

- **High Connectivity:** A smaller diameter suggests a well-linked network where information spreads quickly.
- **Fragmentation:** A very large or increasing diameter indicates that sections of the web are becoming isolated or **fragmented**, making it harder for users (or news aggregator crawlers) to discover content through links alone.

This concept is covered in **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**, specifically regarding graph theory and web connectivity.

highlights.text: explain its relevance in a web graph

parts.content:
(b) Describe one way the concept of web diameter could help the news aggregator refine its results.

[2 marks]
parts.markscheme:
- Web diameter helps identify isolated content clusters/silos that could be better integrated to improve user navigation <marks>1</marks>
- It could guide the aggregator to prioritize or surface links between less connected news sources, creating a more cohesive content network <marks>1</marks>

parts.content:
(c) Explain one limitation of using web diameter to analyze content on the web.

[2 marks]
parts.markscheme:
- Web diameter only measures topological distance between pages; it does not provide insights into the semantic relevance or importance of a page <marks>1</marks>
- It ignores content quality or authority (e.g., PageRank), which means distance alone may not correlate with the usefulness of the search results <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define topological distance
steps.content: Web diameter is a measure used in graph theory to represent the maximum shortest path between any two nodes in a network. In the context of the World Wide Web, it measures the **topological distance**, which is simply the minimum number of clicks (links) required to get from one page to another.

highlights.text: web diameter

steps.title: Explain lack of semantics
steps.content: The primary limitation is that web diameter focuses strictly on the structure of the graph rather than the **semantic relevance** of the pages. It treats every link as having the same value, regardless of whether the content on those pages is contextually related, useful, or authoritative.

steps.title: Analyze the limitation
steps.content:
For a news aggregator, this is a major drawback because two news articles might be 'close' in terms of web diameter (e.g., only two links apart) but have completely different topics. Conversely, two highly relevant articles on the same breaking news story might be 'far apart' topographically if they do not link to each other. 

Therefore, web diameter ignores **content quality and authority**, meaning a shorter distance between pages does not necessarily equate to more relevant or higher-quality search results for the user.

highlights.text: news aggregator

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure your answer includes these two points:
1.  **Topological vs. Semantic:** State that web diameter only measures the physical link structure (topological distance) and lacks insight into the actual meaning or relevance of the content (1 mark).
2.  **Quality and Authority:** Explain that it fails to account for the authority or importance of a page (like PageRank does), so a short link distance doesn't guarantee the content is useful (1 mark).

steps.title: Define web diameter
steps.content: To understand the limitation, we first need to recall what **web diameter** represents. In terms of graph theory, the web is a directed graph where pages are nodes and links are edges. The web diameter is the longest of all shortest paths between any two pages. It is essentially a measure of the "structural size" or **topological distance** of the web.

steps.title: Identify topological vs. semantic
steps.content:
The first major limitation is that web diameter is purely **topological**. It tells us how many 'clicks' away one page is from another, but it provides no insight into **semantic relevance**. 

For example, two pages might be only two clicks apart, but if one is a high-quality news report and the other is a random advertisement, the small distance between them doesn't mean they are related in meaning or importance.

highlights.text: Web diameter only measures topological distance between pages; it does not provide insights into the semantic relevance or importance of a page

steps.title: Focus on content quality
steps.content:
Web diameter ignores **content quality** and **authority**. In search engine algorithms like **PageRank**, a page's value is determined by its authority (how many reputable sources link to it), not just its position in the network. 

A short path between two nodes does not guarantee that the information is useful. A user might find a highly relevant and authoritative source that is mathematically 'far' from their starting point, while a 'close' page might be low-quality spam.

highlights.text: It ignores content quality or authority (e.g., PageRank), which means distance alone may not correlate with the usefulness of the search results

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, your explanation should highlight these two distinct points:
1. **Distance vs. Meaning**: State that diameter only measures the physical link structure (topological distance) and fails to show if the pages are actually relevant to each other.
2. **Quality vs. Connectivity**: Explain that connectivity does not equal authority; a page can be 'close' in terms of diameter but completely useless in terms of content quality.

questions.specification: In a research study, a team of data scientists is using graph theory to analyze connectivity on the web.

parts.content: Explain what is meant by connectivity in the context of the web graph.
parts.markscheme:
- Connectivity refers to the existence and density of hyperlinks connecting different web pages (nodes) within the graph structure; <marks>A1</marks>
- It measures the extent to which pages are reachable from one another, determining the overall navigation potential of the network; <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks total]

steps.title: Define graph components
steps.content:
To understand connectivity using a structural analysis approach, we first define the components of the web graph:

*   **Nodes (Vertices):** Represent individual web pages.
*   **Edges (Arcs):** Represent the hyperlinks that point from one page to another.

This question is covered in **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**, where we treat the World Wide Web as a directed graph.

steps.title: Analyze existence and density
steps.content:
Connectivity refers to the **existence** and **density** of these edges within the graph's topology. 

*   **Existence:** Is there a hyperlink (edge) connecting page $A$ to page $B$?
*   **Density:** How many hyperlinks exist relative to the total number of possible connections in the network?

highlights.text: Connectivity refers to the existence and density of hyperlinks connecting different web pages (nodes) within the graph structure

steps.title: Determine reachability and navigation
steps.content:
Structurally, connectivity determines **reachability**. If a path of edges exists between two nodes, those pages are reachable from one another. 

In practical terms, this defines the **navigation potential** of the web—how easily a user (or a search engine crawler) can move through the network by following links.

highlights.text: It measures the extent to which pages are reachable from one another, determining the overall navigation potential of the network

steps.title: Defining the Graph Structure
steps.content: In the context of the web, **connectivity** first describes the structural relationship between web pages. Within a web graph, individual pages are represented as **nodes** and the hyperlinks between them are **edges**. Connectivity refers to the existence and density of these hyperlinks, indicating how many paths actually exist between different pages.

highlights.text: Connectivity refers to the existence and density of hyperlinks connecting different web pages (nodes) within the graph structure

steps.title: Applying the Reachability Perspective
steps.content:
From a **reachability perspective**, connectivity is defined by **traversal**. It focuses on the functional ability to move from one page to another. 

It measures the extent to which pages are reachable within the network, which directly determines the **navigation potential**. If a graph has high connectivity, a user or a web crawler can easily traverse the network to find information; if it has low connectivity, many pages may become "isolated" or unreachable.

highlights.text: It measures the extent to which pages are reachable from one another, determining the overall navigation potential of the network

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks for this question, your explanation must include:
1.  **Structural definition**: Mentioning hyperlinks connecting nodes and the density of these connections. <marks>A1</marks>
2.  **Reachability definition**: Explaining that it determines how easily pages can be reached and the overall potential for navigation. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Describe one example of how analyzing connectivity could improve website navigation.
parts.markscheme:
- Developers can identify "orphan pages" or isolated sections of a site that have few or no incoming links; <marks>A1</marks>
- By adding strategic internal links to these pages, they can improve the flow of traffic and ensure users find relevant content more easily; <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks total]

steps.title: Identify orphan pages
steps.content: By analyzing the connectivity of a website as a directed graph, developers can identify **orphan pages**. These are isolated nodes that have a zero or very low **in-degree**, meaning they have few or no incoming links from other parts of the site.

highlights.text: Developers can identify "orphan pages" or isolated sections of a site that have few or no incoming links;

steps.title: Integrate with internal links
steps.content: Once identified, navigation is improved by adding strategic **internal links** (edges) from high-traffic pages to these orphans. This integrates them back into the main site structure, ensuring users can discover relevant content and preventing "dead ends" in the user journey.

highlights.text: By adding strategic internal links to these pages, they can improve the flow of traffic and ensure users find relevant content more easily;

steps.title: Model the website's structure
steps.content:
In IB Computer Science **Topic C.5: Analysing the Web**, we learn that the web can be modeled as a **directed graph**. In this model, each web page is a **node**, and each hyperlink is a **directed edge**. 

By viewing the site this way, we can use graph algorithms to measure the 'distance' (number of clicks) between different parts of the site.

steps.title: Identify isolated sections
steps.content: Analyzing connectivity allows developers to identify **"orphan pages"** or isolated sections. These are nodes in the graph that have very few incoming edges, meaning they are difficult for users (and search engine crawlers) to reach from the homepage.

highlights.text: orphan pages

steps.title: Optimize the shortest path
steps.content:
Using the **Shortest Path Optimization** approach, developers calculate the number of clicks required to reach deep content. If a page is too many 'hops' away, they can add **strategic internal links** (new edges in the graph) to create shortcuts.

This reduces the path length, improving the flow of traffic and ensuring users find relevant content more efficiently.

highlights.text: adding strategic internal links

steps.title: Identify the Web as a Graph
steps.content:
In IB Computer Science **Topic C.5 (Analysing the Web)**, we treat the World Wide Web as a **directed graph**. In this graph, web pages are **nodes** and hyperlinks are **edges**. 

To improve navigation, we use **Centrality and Hub Analysis** to determine which pages are key intersections (hubs) and which are isolated from the rest of the site's structure.

steps.title: Locate Low-Centrality 'Orphan' Pages
steps.content:
The first step in this analysis is identifying pages with very low **centrality**. These are often called **"orphan pages"**—sections of a website that have few or no incoming links from other pages. 

Because they lack connectivity, these pages act as dead ends or are simply never reached by users, making them invisible in the site's navigation flow.

highlights.text: identify "orphan pages" or isolated sections of a site that have few or no incoming links

steps.title: Redistribute Links from Hubs
steps.content:
Once isolated pages are found, developers can use **Hub Analysis** to identify the most central, high-traffic pages (hubs). 

By adding **strategic internal links** from these central hubs to the isolated orphan pages, developers "redistribute" the traffic. This ensures that users are efficiently directed toward high-value or relevant content that was previously hard to find.

highlights.text: adding strategic internal links to these pages, they can improve the flow of traffic and ensure users find relevant content more easily

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, your description should include:
1.  **Identification**: Using connectivity analysis to find "orphan" or isolated pages with few incoming links.
2.  **Action/Result**: Adding strategic links from central nodes to these pages to improve traffic flow and user accessibility.

parts.content: Outline two ways in which connectivity insights from graph theory could benefit search engine algorithms.
parts.markscheme:
- **Determining Page Authority**: Algorithms like PageRank analyze the connectivity of a page to determine its importance; <marks>A1</marks>
- Pages with high-quality incoming links from other authoritative hubs are ranked higher, ensuring more relevant results for users; <marks>A1</marks>
- **Improving Crawling Efficiency**: Insights into the graph's link structure allow search engines to identify the most efficient paths for web crawlers; <marks>A1</marks>
- By identifying central hubs that link to many other pages, crawlers can discover and index new or updated content more rapidly; <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award [2] marks for each way outlined, up to a maximum of [4]. Each way should include an identification of the benefit and a brief explanation of how connectivity enables it.</note>

[4 marks total]

steps.title: Understanding the Web Graph
steps.content: In graph theory, we model the web as a **directed graph**. Within this model, web pages are represented as **nodes** (or vertices) and the hyperlinks connecting them are represented as **edges** (or arcs). By analyzing the properties of this graph, search engines can significantly improve how they find and rank information. We will look at two specific applications: **Page Authority** and **Crawling Efficiency**.

highlights.text: graph theory to analyze connectivity on the web

steps.title: Application 1: Determining Page Authority
steps.content:
The first major benefit is determining **Page Authority** (often associated with the PageRank algorithm).

*   **Identification**: Algorithms analyze the connectivity (the number and quality of incoming links) to determine a page's importance.
*   **Explanation**: In the web graph, an incoming edge is essentially a "vote" of confidence. If a page receives many links from other highly authoritative "hubs," it is ranked higher in search results. This ensures that users see the most relevant and trustworthy content first.

highlights.text: Determining Page Authority
high-quality incoming links from other authoritative hubs are ranked higher

steps.title: Application 2: Improving Crawling Efficiency
steps.content:
The second benefit involves **Improving Crawling Efficiency** through graph traversal.

*   **Identification**: Graph theory insights help search engines identify the most efficient paths for web crawlers (bots that index the web).
*   **Explanation**: By identifying **central hubs** (nodes with a high degree of connectivity), crawlers can discover a vast number of new or updated pages very quickly. Instead of wandering randomly, the crawler uses the graph structure to prioritize paths that lead to the most "connected" parts of the web, speeding up the indexing process.

highlights.text: Improving Crawling Efficiency
identifying central hubs that link to many other pages

steps.title: Final Mark Distribution
steps.content:
To achieve full marks in an IB exam, you must provide a clear identification and a brief explanation for each way. 

$$\text{Total Marks} = 2 \text{ ways} \times 2 \text{ marks each} = 4$$

*   **1 mark** for identifying the benefit (e.g., Page Authority).
*   **1 mark** for explaining how connectivity/links enable that benefit (e.g., authoritative links lead to higher rankings).

questions.specification: A web search engine uses graph theory to analyze its network of interconnected web pages.

parts.content: Describe the term strongly connected core (SCC) in the context of a web graph.
parts.markscheme:
A strongly connected core (SCC) is a subgraph where every node (web page) can be reached from every other node within the same subgraph. <marks>A1</marks>

In the context of the web, it represents a central cluster of pages with high interlinking density, often containing the most authoritative and frequently visited sites. <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks total]

steps.title: Identify the web graph
steps.content:
To understand the **Strongly Connected Core (SCC)**, we first treat the World Wide Web as a **directed graph**. 

In this model:
- **Nodes** represent individual web pages.
- **Directed edges** represent the hyperlinks connecting them.

This is covered in **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**, where we look at how graph theory helps search engines map the internet.

steps.title: Define mutual reachability
steps.content:
From a formal graph theory perspective, an SCC is a subgraph defined by **mutual reachability**. 

This means that for any two web pages $A$ and $B$ within that subgraph, there is a path from $A$ to $B$ **and** a path from $B$ to $A$. In other words, every page in the SCC can reach every other page by following a sequence of links. This is worth the first mark. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Contextualize for web navigation
steps.content:
In the context of the web, the SCC represents a **central cluster** of highly interconnected pages. 

Because these pages are so densely linked to one another, they often contain the most authoritative and frequently visited sites on the internet (like major news outlets or search engines). Navigation within this core is easy because you can effectively travel between any two sites in the cluster just by clicking links. This satisfies the second mark. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define SCC reachability
steps.content: In graph theory, a **Strongly Connected Core (SCC)** is defined by its internal reachability. Within this specific subgraph, every node (representing a web page) must have a directed path to every other node in that same subgraph. This means you can start at any page in the SCC and, by following links, eventually arrive at any other page within the core.

highlights.text: every node (web page) can be reached from every other node within the same subgraph

steps.title: Analyze the bow-tie model
steps.content: From a structural perspective, the SCC is the heart of the "bow-tie" model of the web. It is a high-density cluster characterized by intense interconnectivity. Because these pages link to each other so frequently, they often represent the most authoritative and frequently visited sites on the internet, acting as the central hub that connects the 'IN' components (pages linking to the core) to the 'OUT' components (pages the core links to).

highlights.text: central cluster of pages with high interlinking density

steps.title: Identify mark-earning points
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must provide two distinct points:
1. The **topological definition**: Mention that every page can reach every other page within the subgraph (1 mark).
2. The **web context**: Explain that it is a central, high-density cluster containing authoritative or popular sites (1 mark).

This concept is covered in **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**. If you are looking to visualize this, imagine a dense knot of strings where every string is tied to every other string in the middle of a larger web.

parts.content: Outline two benefits of identifying the SCC within a web graph for search engines.
parts.markscheme:
**Benefit 1: Crawl Optimization**
Search engines can prioritize crawling the SCC to ensure the most interconnected and significant pages are indexed first. <marks>A1</marks> This improves the freshness of search results by focusing resources on the most relevant parts of the web. <marks>A1</marks>

**Benefit 2: Authority and Ranking**
Pages within the SCC are generally more authoritative because they belong to a mutually reinforcing link structure. <marks>A1</marks> Identifying these pages helps search algorithms (like PageRank) more accurately assign weight and rank to popular websites. <marks>A1</marks>

[4 marks total]

steps.title: Define the SCC property
steps.content:
To understand the benefits for a search engine, we first need to define a **Strongly Connected Component (SCC)**. In a directed graph like the web, an SCC is a subset of pages where **every page is reachable from every other page** in that same subset.

This mathematical property of **mutual reachability** implies that these pages are tightly knit and likely contain the most significant content on the web.

steps.title: Benefit 1: Crawl Optimization
steps.content:
Search engines use "crawlers" to discover and index pages. Identifying the SCC allows the search engine to **prioritize crawling** this specific group of pages.

*   **Conceptual Link:** Because every page in an SCC is mutually reachable, a crawler can move efficiently between them without getting stuck in "dead ends."
*   **Operational Benefit:** By indexing this core first, the search engine ensures its results remain **fresh and relevant**, focusing its limited computing resources on the most interconnected parts of the web.

This earns **2 marks**: one for identifying crawl optimization and one for explaining the improvement in result freshness.

highlights.text: Search engines can prioritize crawling the SCC to ensure the most interconnected and significant pages are indexed first.
This improves the freshness of search results by focusing resources on the most relevant parts of the web.

steps.title: Benefit 2: Authority and Ranking
steps.content:
The second benefit relates to how a search engine decides which pages are "better" than others (ranking).

*   **Conceptual Link:** Pages within an SCC exhibit high **link density**. They essentially "vouch" for one another through a mutually reinforcing link structure.
*   **Operational Benefit:** Search algorithms, such as **PageRank**, use these link structures to determine **authority**. A page within a large SCC is statistically more likely to be a high-quality, popular site than an isolated page.

This earns the final **2 marks**: one for identifying authority/ranking and one for explaining how algorithms like PageRank use this structure to assign weight.

highlights.text: Pages within the SCC are generally more authoritative because they belong to a mutually reinforcing link structure.
Identifying these pages helps search algorithms (like PageRank) more accurately assign weight and rank to popular websites.

steps.title: Summary for Markscheme
steps.content:
To get full marks on this question, ensure your response connects the graph property to a practical search engine requirement:

1.  **Crawl Efficiency:** Mention that mutual reachability allows for prioritizing the index to keep results "fresh."
2.  **Authority Ranking:** Mention that the dense link structure helps algorithms like PageRank identify significant or "popular" websites.

parts.content: Explain one way in which the concept of web diameter helps search engines refine search results.
parts.markscheme:
The web diameter is defined as the maximum shortest path between any two nodes in the web graph. <marks>A1</marks>

Knowing the diameter allows search engines to understand the global scale of connectivity and set efficient crawling depth limits. <marks>M1</marks>

This prevents crawlers from entering potentially infinite loops or dynamic "spider traps" while ensuring they navigate deep enough to capture most of the relevant, reachable content, leading to a cleaner and more representative search index. <marks>R1</marks>

[3 marks total]

steps.title: Define the web diameter
steps.content:
To understand how search engines refine results, we first need to define the **web diameter**. In graph theory, the web is viewed as a collection of nodes (pages) and edges (links). The diameter is the **maximum shortest path** between any two nodes in that graph.

This concept is part of **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**. If you're comfortable with basic graph theory, you'll remember that the diameter tells us the "longest distance" one would ever have to travel to get from one page to another using the most direct route possible.

steps.title: Set crawling depth limits
steps.content:
Using the **Crawling Depth Optimization** approach, search engines use this diameter to establish a mathematical limit for their web crawlers (spiders). 

If a search engine knows the diameter of the web graph is $D$, it can set a crawling depth limit near $D$. This ensures that the crawler navigates deep enough into the network to capture all relevant and reachable content without wandering aimlessly. Without this limit, a crawler wouldn't know when to stop searching "downwards" from a starting page.

steps.title: Avoid traps and loops
steps.content:
The main way this refines results is by identifying and avoiding **spider traps** or infinite loops. These are areas of the web (often dynamically generated) that create a never-ending series of links.

By applying a depth limit based on the web diameter, the search engine can:
1. **Prevent infinite loops**: Stop the crawler once it exceeds a reasonable depth.
2. **Improve index quality**: Ensure the index isn't filled with junk data from dynamic traps.
3. **Efficiently reach content**: Focus resources on finding genuine, reachable pages that are within the known "scale" of the web.

steps.title: Define the Web Diameter
steps.content:
To understand how search engines refine results, we first need to define our benchmark: the **web diameter**. In graph theory, the web is viewed as a collection of nodes (pages) and edges (links). The diameter is defined as the maximum shortest path between any two nodes in the web graph. 

This gives the search engine a mathematical "expected maximum distance" between any two legitimate pages.

highlights.text: maximum shortest path between any two nodes

steps.title: Establish Structural Integrity
steps.content: By knowing the diameter, search engines can establish **structural integrity** benchmarks. They use this value to set efficient crawling depth limits. If a web crawler follows a sequence of links that far exceeds the known web diameter, it suggests that the structure it is navigating is likely artificial or anomalous rather than a standard part of the interconnected web.

highlights.text: set efficient crawling depth limits

steps.title: Detect and Filter Traps
steps.content:
This benchmark is crucial for **trap detection**. Artificial structures, such as "link farms" or dynamic "spider traps," are designed to keep crawlers in infinite loops or extremely long paths to artificially boost SEO. 

When a search engine identifies a path that violates the expected web diameter, it can:
1. Stop the crawler from wasting resources.
2. Filter these anomalous pages out of the index.

This results in a cleaner, more representative search index for the user.

highlights.text: spider traps
cleaner and more representative search index

questions.specification: The structure of the web can be represented using graph theory, where nodes represent web pages and edges represent hyperlinks between them.

parts.content: Define a directed graph and describe its relevance to representing the structure of the web.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** a directed graph as a collection of nodes (vertices) and directed edges (arcs) that connect them in a specific direction <marks>A1</marks>

- **Identify** that in this context, nodes represent web pages and directed edges represent hyperlinks pointing from one page to another <marks>A1</marks>

- **Describe** the relevance by stating that the directed edges illustrate the possible navigational paths and the overall connectivity of the web <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define the Directed Graph
steps.content:
A **directed graph** (or digraph) is a mathematical structure defined as a set of **nodes** (or vertices) and a set of **directed edges** (or arcs). 

Mathematically, it can be represented as:
$$G = (V, E)$$

Where:
- $V$ is a set of vertices.
- $E$ is a set of ordered pairs of vertices, where an edge $(u, v)$ indicates a specific direction from node $u$ to node $v$.

steps.title: Map Graph to Web
steps.content:
Using the **Standard Conceptual Mapping** approach, we can translate these mathematical components into the context of the World Wide Web:

- **Nodes (Vertices):** Each node represents an individual **web page** (identified by its URL).
- **Directed Edges (Arcs):** Each edge represents a **hyperlink**. If Page A has a link that points to Page B, there is a directed edge originating at node A and terminating at node B.

highlights.text: nodes represent web pages and edges represent hyperlinks

steps.title: Explain Navigational Relevance
steps.content:
Representing the web this way is crucial for understanding its **navigational connectivity**. 

By looking at the directed edges, we can determine the possible paths a user or a web crawler can take through the internet. This structure allows us to identify "hubs" (pages with many outgoing links) and "authorities" (pages with many incoming links), which is exactly how search engines analyze the importance of pages. 

You can explore this further in **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**.

steps.title: Final Mark Breakdown
steps.content:
To ensure you get full marks, remember these three components:
1.  **Definition:** Nodes and directed edges with a specific direction (1 mark).
2.  **Mapping:** Nodes = web pages; Edges = hyperlinks (1 mark).
3.  **Relevance:** It illustrates the navigational paths and overall connectivity of the web (1 mark).

parts.content: Explain one benefit of representing the web as a directed graph.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** that graph representation allows for the analysis of interconnectivity, such as identifying central or high-authority nodes through algorithms like PageRank <marks>M1</marks>

- **Explain** that this information allows search engines to rank pages by importance or helps developers optimize the distribution of links to improve searchability <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze interconnectivity and authority
steps.content:
Representing the web as a directed graph allows us to analyze the **interconnectivity** between pages. Because edges have direction (from a source page to a destination page), we can identify which pages act as "hubs" or "authorities."

In the context of the **PageRank algorithm**, every incoming link (in-link) to a page is essentially a "vote" of confidence. By analyzing these directed edges, search engines can identify high-authority nodes that are referenced frequently by other reputable sources.

highlights.text: identify central or high-authority nodes through algorithms like PageRank

steps.title: Improving search engine ranking
steps.content:
Once the authority of each node is determined using the directed graph structure, search engines can use this information to **rank pages by importance**.

Instead of just looking for keywords, the engine can deliver results that are mathematically proven to be more credible or relevant based on their position within the graph. This significantly improves the quality of search results for the user and helps developers understand how to optimize link distribution to improve a site's searchability.

highlights.text: rank pages by importance or helps developers optimize the distribution of links to improve searchability

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To earn both marks, you must first identify that the graph allows for **authority analysis** via algorithms like PageRank ($M1$), and then explain that this enables search engines to **rank results by importance** ($A1$).

This concept is a key part of **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**. If you want to dive deeper into how these calculations work, review the section on the **Page Rank Algorithm**.

steps.title: Define the graph structure
steps.content: To understand the benefit, we first look at how the web is modeled. In this directed graph, each **node** represents a web page and each **directed edge** represents a hyperlink. Because hyperlinks are one-way (clicking a link on Page A takes you to Page B, but Page B doesn't necessarily link back), the graph must be directed to accurately map the path a search engine spider takes.

highlights.text: nodes represent web pages and edges represent hyperlinks between them

steps.title: Analyze interconnectivity and authority
steps.content: The main benefit of this representation is that it allows for the **analysis of interconnectivity**. By examining the direction and number of edges, we can identify **high-authority nodes** (pages that many other pages link to). Algorithms like **PageRank** use this graph data to determine which pages are the most central or influential in the network. This earns the first mark ($M1$).

steps.title: Improve search and ranking
steps.content: Once the search engine identifies these authoritative nodes through graph traversal, it can **rank pages by importance** in search results. This ensures that when a user searches for a term, the most relevant and trusted pages (those with high connectivity in the graph) appear first. This explanation of how the data is used for ranking earns the second mark ($A1$).

parts.content: Outline one limitation of using a directed graph to analyze the web.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that the web is a highly dynamic environment where pages and hyperlinks are constantly being added, modified, or removed <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explain** that a static graph representation quickly becomes outdated, leading to an inaccurate model of the current web structure <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Recognize the web's dynamic nature
steps.content: The first point to understand is that the World Wide Web is not a fixed structure. It is a **highly dynamic environment** where content is in a constant state of flux. Every second, new web pages are published, existing pages are modified, and old pages are deleted. Similarly, the hyperlinks (edges) connecting these pages are frequently added or broken.

steps.title: Identify the static limitation
steps.content:
When we represent the web as a directed graph, we are essentially taking a "snapshot" of it at a specific point in time. However, because the web changes so rapidly, this **static graph representation** cannot keep up with real-time developments. 

In IB Computer Science, this is a classic example of a model failing to reflect a changing reality—a concept also touched upon in **Topic 1.1.2: The Importance of Change Management**.

steps.title: Explain the resulting inaccuracy
steps.content:
Because the graph is static while the web is dynamic, the model quickly becomes **outdated**. This leads to inaccuracies in analysis; for example, a search engine algorithm might direct a user to a "node" (page) that no longer exists or miss a new, highly relevant "edge" (link) that was just created. 

To earn full marks, you must connect the state of the web (dynamic) to the failure of the model (becoming obsolete).

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks, your answer should look like this:

*   **State (A1):** The web is a highly dynamic environment where pages and links are constantly added, removed, or modified.
*   **Explain (R1):** Therefore, a static directed graph becomes outdated almost immediately, leading to an inaccurate model of the actual web structure.

highlights.text: Outline one limitation of using a directed graph to analyze the web.

steps.title: Contextualize the graph model
steps.content:
In **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**, we learn that the web can be modeled as a directed graph where:
- The set of vertices $V$ represents individual web pages.
- The set of directed edges $E$ represents the hyperlinks connecting them.

While this is a powerful mathematical tool, the primary limitation when applying it to the actual World Wide Web is its overwhelming **scale**.

highlights.text: nodes represent web pages and edges represent hyperlinks

steps.title: Identify the scale
steps.content:
The first part of the limitation is the sheer size of the data set. The modern web contains **billions of nodes** (pages) and an even higher number of directed edges (links). 

To represent this as a graph, a system would need to store and manage a massive adjacency list or matrix that accounts for every single $v \in V$ and $e \in E$ across the entire globe.

steps.title: Explain the computational burden
steps.content:
Because of this immense complexity, the computational resources required to process the graph are enormous. 

1. **Storage:** Keeping a complete, high-resolution map of the web requires petabytes of memory.
2. **Processing:** Running algorithms (like PageRank) on a graph with billions of edges is extremely time-consuming and computationally expensive. 

By the time a complete directed graph is processed and analyzed, the actual structure of the web has likely already changed, making the static graph model resource-intensive and impractical for real-time analysis.

highlights.text: Outline one limitation of using a directed graph to analyze the web.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn both marks in an exam using this approach, you should structure your answer as follows:

- **State (1 mark):** The web consists of billions of pages and links, creating a graph of massive scale.
- **Explain (1 mark):** The computational power and storage required to process such a complex directed graph make a complete representation impractical or highly resource-intensive.

steps.title: Identify the model's scope
steps.content:
To answer this, we need to look at what a directed graph actually represents. In **Topic C.5: Analysing the web**, we learn that search engines build these graphs by following hyperlinks. 

This means the graph is built solely on **indexed** or **reachable** pages. Any page that doesn't have a public link pointing to it cannot be included in the model.

highlights.text: directed graph to analyze the web

steps.title: The Incompleteness Argument
steps.content:
The core limitation here is the **Incompleteness Argument**. A directed graph fails to account for the **"Deep Web"**—the vast portion of the internet that is not indexed by standard search engines. 

This includes:
- Pages protected by **authentication** (logins)
- Content behind **paywalls**
- Private databases or unlinked files

Since these nodes and edges are hidden, the graph cannot represent them.

steps.title: Explain the inaccuracy
steps.content:
Because the directed graph only captures the "Surface Web," it provides an **incomplete and inaccurate model** of the web's true structure.
